topics.title: Mod root layout and loadable folders
topics.body: A HOI4 mod is a mirror of selected game folders. Generated content should be placed under game-readable directories such as common, events, history, interface, gfx, map, music, sound, and localisation. A batch tool must treat paths as relative to the mod root and reject absolute paths or traversal.
relationships: Descriptor files advertise metadata; replace_path changes load semantics; localisation keys must match script IDs.
validation: Reject paths outside the mod root.
Prefer stable feature-level filenames.
Keep generated script and localisation in separate files.

topics.title: descriptor.mod and launcher metadata
topics.body: The descriptor controls launcher metadata, supported version, tags, dependencies, and optional path replacement. Generators should not add replace_path unless the user explicitly wants to replace an entire vanilla folder, because it can hide vanilla content.
relationships: replace_path affects every generated file in the replaced folder.
supported_version relates to latest-update resources.
validation: Warn on replace_path by default.
Keep descriptor strings quoted.
Do not infer Steam workshop metadata unless provided.

topics.title: Load order, replace_path, and file precedence
topics.body: HOI4 loads multiple files in many folders, while some folders or filenames effectively override content. replace_path is a descriptor-level override and should be treated as high risk. Generated files should minimize conflicts by using unique IDs and mod-specific filenames.
relationships: Conflicts are often visible in error.log or by missing vanilla content.
Namespace and ID strategy reduce conflicts.
validation: Avoid writing generic filenames like 00_common.txt unless intentionally overriding.
Use mod prefixes for generated IDs.

topics.title: Paradox script structure
topics.body: HOI4 script is a key/value and nested block language. Whitespace is flexible, but readable indentation matters for review. Generators must balance braces, avoid duplicate equals signs, preserve quoted strings, and use comments only where they clarify non-obvious logic.
relationships: Triggers, effects, modifiers, and data definitions share this grammar.
GUI and GFX files use related block syntax with different keywords.
validation: Count braces before writing.
Format with tabs for HOI4 script readability.
Keep generated IDs ASCII and stable.

topics.title: Scopes and scope transitions
topics.body: Scopes determine what an effect or trigger reads. ROOT, THIS, FROM, and PREV can change meaning inside nested blocks. Generated chains must explicitly understand whether they are operating on a country, state, character, unit leader, strategic region, or array element.
syntax_blocks: random_owned_state = { add_building_construction = { type = industrial_complex level = 1 instant_build = yes } }
relationships: Effects and triggers are valid only in compatible scopes.
Events and on_actions often introduce FROM or ROOT relationships.
validation: Annotate expected root scope in generated plans.
Do not nest country-only effects inside state scopes without transition.
Use temporary variables for complex scope calculations.

topics.title: Triggers and condition blocks
topics.body: Triggers are boolean conditions used in availability, visibility, AI logic, scripted triggers, decisions, focuses, events, and GUI. They should be scoped correctly and kept readable. Complex conditions should be grouped under AND, OR, NOT, limit, or scripted triggers.
relationships: Triggers gate effects; scripted_triggers reuse trigger logic; GUI triggers can compute temp variables before properties consume them.
validation: Check scope compatibility.
Use always = yes/no only when intentional.
Avoid expensive visible triggers in frequently refreshed GUI.

topics.title: Effects and state mutation
topics.body: Effects mutate game state. They appear in focus rewards, event options, decisions, on_actions, scripted effects, history setup, and GUI clicks. Generated effects should be minimal, scoped, and idempotent when possible.
relationships: Effects are guarded by triggers; scripted_effects share reusable effect chains; on_actions run effects in event-driven contexts.
validation: Check whether an effect is country, state, character, or unit scoped.
Avoid repeated additive effects unless the trigger prevents repetition.
Name flags and variables with mod prefixes.
Hide helper-only triggers, effects, or calculation modifiers from player-facing UI whenever the file type supports a hidden form.

topics.title: Modifiers and modifier scopes
topics.body: Modifiers alter values rather than directly running effects. They are used in ideas, traits, states, technologies, equipment, decisions, dynamic modifiers, opinion modifiers, and scripted contexts. Generated modifiers must use valid modifier keys for the target owner.
relationships: Ideas and traits wrap modifiers; dynamic modifiers are attached by effects; technologies and equipment use modifiers for gameplay values.
validation: Do not mix state-only and country-only modifiers.
Prefer small numeric values with explicit sign.
Use HOI4DOC or wiki lookup before adding unfamiliar modifier keys.
Hide helper-only triggers, effects, or calculation modifiers from player-facing UI whenever the file type supports a hidden form.

topics.title: Variables, temp variables, and math
topics.body: Variables store numbers on scopes; temp variables exist for temporary calculations. GUI, scripted effects, scripted triggers, decisions, and events can use variables for dynamic systems. Prefer explicit variable prefixes and clean temp names.
relationships: Arrays can be built from variables; GUI properties can read temp variables; MTTH and scripted localisation can reference variables.
validation: Use temp_ prefix for temp variables.
Reset temporary arrays before rebuilding.
Avoid unprefixed global variable names.

topics.title: MTTH variables and dynamic scripted values
topics.body: MTTH variables are named dynamic numeric values, usually defined under common/mtth/*.txt, that can be referenced as mtth:<name> in script contexts that accept variable-like values. Do not confuse them with an event mean_time_to_happen block: event MTTH decides when a non-triggered event is checked, while a common/mtth value is a reusable score/value expression. A generator should define the MTTH variable with an explicit base, then apply ordered modifier blocks with factor/add plus triggers, and copy the result into set_temp_variable when later math, comparisons, GUI properties, or scripted effects need a stable value.
syntax_blocks: my_mod_support_score = { base = 0 modifier = { add = 5 has_war = yes } modifier = { factor = 0 NOT = { has_country_flag = my_mod_system_enabled } } }
mean_time_to_happen = { days = 30 modifier = { factor = 0 has_country_flag = my_mod_event_blocked } }
relationships: MTTH blocks use the same score grammar as ai_will_do and many priority blocks: base/add/factor plus trigger-style modifier blocks.
Variables and temp variables can consume mtth:<name> values, then clamp, compare, or display them.
Decision priorities, focus AI weights, event MTTH, and scripted GUI calculations often need the same base/modifier mental model.
validation: Document expected input scope for each MTTH variable; ROOT/THIS/FROM assumptions affect triggers inside modifier blocks.
Use a mod prefix for MTTH IDs and temp variables.
Keep modifier order intentional because multiple matching modifiers are applied in file order.
Use set_temp_variable before reusing the value more than once in a generated block.
Verify uncommon mtth:<name> consumers against the current game documentation before release-critical generation.

topics.title: Unique identifiers, creation intent, and duplicate checks
topics.body: Before creating a new role, scope-aware flag, TAG, idea token, dynamic modifier, focus, decision, event namespace, scripted effect, scripted trigger, character, or other reusable identifier, agents should run a structured uniqueness scan across the user's mod root and any configured game root. Only creation intent should be treated as a duplicate risk; when the user asks to reference existing content, the same match is informational. Focus IDs need special handling: focus = { id = ... }, shared_focus = { id = ... }, joint_focus = { id = ... }, and shared_focus/joint_focus references are focus ID matches, while focus_tree = { id = ... } is a focus tree ID and must not be confused with a focus node ID. Flag checks should distinguish country_flag, global_flag, state_flag, character_flag, mio_flag, and project_flag; unit_leader_flag is treated as the legacy character flag family. Generated file names, folder names, script identifiers, idea tokens, focus IDs, event IDs, variables, flags, OOB division names, and similar machine-readable tokens should stay ASCII-only; localisation prose and visible player text are the normal exceptions.
syntax_blocks: scan_unique_identifiers roots=[{ path=<MOD_ROOT>, role=mod }, { path=<GAME_ROOT>, role=game }] candidates=[{ entity_type=focus_id, value=TAG_reform_army, intent=create }]
relationships: Same-file overwrite risk is separate from identifier duplication; planned output paths should be checked against the mod root before writing.
descriptor.mod and .mod replace_path declarations can hide vanilla folders and should be surfaced whenever planned paths sit below replaced folders.
Country tags are defined structurally in common/country_tags, not by every textual appearance of TAG.

Flags and variables are usage-defined in script and should be scanned through structured effect/trigger keys such as set_country_flag, has_character_flag, modify_mio_flag, set_project_flag, set_variable, and check_variable.
validation: Do not use plain global text search for uniqueness decisions.
Batch multiple candidates in one scan so a model can check a whole focus branch or idea set before writing files.
Report exact path, line, kind, and scope of each match.
Treat game-root matches as compatibility/override risks and mod-root matches as duplicate or overwrite risks.
When a duplicate is intentional, change intent to reference or ask the user to confirm an override.
Reject non-ASCII names for workspace paths and token-like fields unless the field is explicitly player-facing localisation text.

topics.title: Arrays and data structures
topics.body: Arrays store scope or value lists and enable generated dynamic content, especially scripted GUI dynamic_lists and repeated script operations. Arrays need clear owner scope and reset behavior.
relationships: Dynamic GUI lists iterate arrays; scripted effects can populate arrays; variables can be read by index.
validation: Clear temp arrays before rebuilding.
Define index/value names in dynamic_lists.
Check whether array values are scopes or numbers.

topics.title: Flags and one-time state gates
topics.body: Flags are lightweight state markers for one-time effects, event chains, GUI state, and decision unlocks. Use them for boolean state, but prefer variables when numeric state or dynamic UI frame calculation is needed.
relationships: Flags gate triggers; events set flags; decisions and focuses read flags.
validation: Prefix flag names.
Avoid flags for values that should be variables.
Clear temporary flags when they represent transient state.

topics.title: Localisation encoding and key format
topics.body: HOI4 localisation yml files should be written with UTF-8 BOM. A file starts with a language root such as l_english: or l_simp_chinese:. Entries use key:0 "Text". Keep generated keys stable and match script identifiers where possible. Localisation is player-facing writing, not a placeholder channel: generated focus descriptions, mission text, event text, and UI text should be complete prose in the target language. If the project already has narrative or promotional prose, summarize its style and imitate it before writing new entries.
relationships: Script IDs usually become localisation keys; tools writing yml must preserve BOM; scripted localisation can compose dynamic text.
Hidden triggers, hidden effects, and calculation-only modifiers should not leak into localisation as visible design notes.
validation: Write UTF-8 BOM for yml.
Keep one language root per file.
Escape quotes in generated strings.
Do not write TODO, placeholder, draft, or design-spec wording as final localisation.
For focus descriptions, missions, and events, provide polished prose unless the user explicitly requests terse text.
Keep script keys ASCII even when the visible value is Chinese, Japanese, Russian, or another non-ASCII language.

topics.title: Dynamic localisation, formatting, and icons
topics.body: Localisation can reference scripted localisation, variables, icons, and formatting tokens. Generators should separate static display text from scripted dynamic branches and ensure every scripted localisation key has a fallback.
relationships: scripted_localisation.entries defines dynamic branches.
GUI textboxes and events consume localisation keys.
validation: Check bracket balance.
Provide fallback text.
Do not embed untranslated script IDs in visible text.

topics.title: Scripted triggers and scripted effects
topics.body: Scripted triggers and effects are reusable named blocks. They reduce duplication across events, focuses, decisions, and GUI effects. They should be written with clear scope assumptions and parameter-like naming conventions where supported by the target syntax.
relationships: Triggers return boolean; effects mutate state.
Focuses, decisions, events, and GUI can call these named blocks.
validation: Document expected scope in comments.
Do not hide side effects in trigger-like names.
Keep reusable blocks small.

topics.title: Scripted localisation entries
topics.body: Scripted localisation chooses text by triggers. It is useful for state-dependent labels, GUI strings, event descriptions, and dynamic tooltips. Keep branch order deterministic and provide a default branch.
syntax_blocks: defined_text = { name = GetMyStatus text = { trigger = { has_war = yes } localization_key = my_status_war } text = { localization_key = my_status_peace } }
relationships: Localisation yml stores the visible keys; triggers choose which key is active.
validation: Provide an unconditional fallback.
Keep branch triggers cheap for frequently refreshed GUI text.
Ensure every localization_key exists.

topics.title: National focus trees
topics.body: National focus files define trees, focus nodes, prerequisites, availability, bypass logic, AI weights, and completion rewards. Generated branches need stable IDs, positions, icons, and matching localisation. Vanilla focus spacing is only a fallback. Before adding or reshaping a focus tree, inspect the workspace and dependency mods for interface/nationalfocusview.gui. If it exists, derive safe spacing from focus_spacing, positionType, and the actual focus icon dimensions instead of assuming x=2 and y=1. If the project state is ambiguous, ask the user before laying out a tree that could overlap.
syntax_blocks: focus_tree = { id = TAG_01 country = { factor = 0 modifier = { add = 10 tag = TAG } } focus = { id = TAG_01_FS_00 x = 0 y = 0 cost = 10 completion_reward = { add_political_power = 50 } } }
relationships: Focus IDs are localisation keys and icon suffixes.
AI weights use triggers.
Rewards are effects.
Custom nationalfocusview.gui changes how x/y focus coordinates map to pixels and can make vanilla spacing unsafe.
validation: Check position overlap.
Use prerequisites or mutually_exclusive for branches.
Keep focus IDs unique across mods.
Use x=2/y=1 spacing only when no custom nationalfocusview.gui is present.
When custom focus UI exists, calculate overlap from focus_spacing and icon dimensions before writing positions.
Ask the user when dependency mods or GUI overrides make focus spacing ambiguous.

topics.title: Shared focuses, filters, and AI focus behavior
topics.body: Focus trees can use shared focuses, filters, bypasses, and AI weights to manage large trees. Generators should represent focus intent separately from layout so AI and branch constraints can be generated consistently.
syntax_blocks: ai_will_do = { factor = 1 modifier = { factor = 0 has_war = yes } }
relationships: AI weights consume triggers.
Shared focus definitions interact with tree-specific placement.
validation: Avoid AI factor = 0 unless intentionally disabling.
Keep filters aligned with reward type.
Do not generate impossible prerequisite chains.

topics.title: Country events
topics.body: Country events are namespaced scripted events with title, desc, trigger, immediate effects, and options. They are used for narrative chains, decision outcomes, focus rewards, and on_action responses.
syntax_blocks: country_event = { id = my_mod.1 title = my_mod.1.t desc = my_mod.1.d is_triggered_only = yes option = { name = my_mod.1.a } }
relationships: Options run effects; localisation keys provide text; on_actions and focuses fire events.
validation: Use namespaces.
Ensure option names are localised.
Avoid missing is_triggered_only when event should not pulse.

topics.title: News events and MTTH
topics.body: News events target global presentation while MTTH-driven events evaluate over time. Generated events must avoid accidental spam and must distinguish triggered-only chains from recurring pulse logic.
syntax_blocks: news_event = { id = my_mod.10 title = my_mod.10.t desc = my_mod.10.d major = yes }
mean_time_to_happen = { days = 30 modifier = { factor = 0 has_war = no } }
relationships: MTTH uses triggers and modifiers.
News events need localisation and pictures.
validation: Gate recurring events tightly.
Use fire_only_once when appropriate.
Check picture keys.

topics.title: Event chains and delayed follow-up
topics.body: Event chains pass context through scopes and delayed event calls. Generators need to preserve ROOT/FROM expectations and avoid losing the original actor when chaining through states or characters.
relationships: Scope handling controls who receives follow-up events.
Flags often prevent duplicate branches.
validation: Document sender and receiver scope.
Set flags before delayed events if repetition matters.
Localise every branch.

topics.title: On actions and pulse hooks
topics.body: On actions attach events or effects to hardcoded game hooks. Files live under common/on_actions/*.txt and use on_actions = { hook_name = { effect = { ... } random_events = { ... } } }. effect is an effect block and is valid for every hook. random_events can be used when the default hook scope is country-scoped and preserves the hook's ROOT/FROM/FROM.FROM chain for the fired event. Multiple random_events blocks each pick separately. on_actions do not fire from history/bookmark setup before the game has started.
syntax_blocks: on_actions = { on_startup = { effect = { ENG = { country_event = { id = my_mod.1 days = 30 } } } } }
relationships: ROOT is the assumed default scope unless the hook documents THIS separately from ROOT.
FROM and FROM.FROM are secondary scopes supplied by the hook, not universal meanings.
random_events should be weighted with 0 entries when no event should fire for most checks.
Pulse hooks should call events or scripted effects with cheap trigger gates instead of doing broad work inline.
validation: Always include effect = { ... } when writing direct effects; otherwise the block can be confused with random_events-style content.
Avoid heavy logic directly in frequent pulses.
Use event triggers or if = { limit = { ... } } to filter.
Do not add global hooks without a clear mod prefix and documented scope table.
For release-critical code, verify hook existence and scope notes against the current game documentation files.

topics.title: On action hook families and scope matrix
topics.body: This index links detailed on_action families. THIS, ROOT, FROM, FROM.FROM, PREV, and OWNER are hook-specific and cannot be assigned one global meaning. General and pulse hooks: on_startup, on_daily, on_daily_TAG, on_weekly, on_weekly_TAG, on_monthly, on_monthly_TAG. Politics and peace hooks: on_stage_coup, on_coup_succeeded, on_government_change, on_ruling_party_change, on_new_term_election, on_before_peace_conference_start, on_peaceconference_started, on_peaceconference_ended. Diplomacy and war hooks: on_send_volunteers, on_border_war_lost, on_war_relation_added, on_declare_war, on_war, on_peace, on_capitulation, on_capitulation_immediate, on_uncapitulation, on_annex, on_civil_war_end_before_annexation, on_civil_war_end, on_puppet, on_liberate, on_release_as_free, on_release_as_puppet, on_guarantee, on_military_access, on_offer_military_access, on_call_allies, on_join_allies, on_lend_lease, on_incoming_lend_lease, on_send_expeditionary_force, on_return_expeditionary_forces, on_request_expeditionary_forces, on_ask_for_state_control, on_give_state_control, on_peace_proposal, on_send_attache. Faction/autonomy/exile hooks, state/wargoal hooks, unit leader/military/ace hooks, and La Resistance/MIO hooks are listed in their own topics.
relationships: THIS, ROOT, FROM, FROM.FROM, PREV, OWNER are hook-specific and must be documented per generated hook.
random_events inherits the on_action scope chain for event trigger evaluation.
on_state_control_changed is the common example where ROOT is new controller, FROM is old controller, and FROM.FROM is the state.
on_naval_invasion and on_paradrop are examples where THIS is not ROOT.
validation: Before generating any OA file, write the hook name, category, default scope, ROOT, FROM, FROM.FROM, PREV, and OWNER assumptions when applicable.
If a hook is absent from this local snapshot, do not invent scope meaning; require current-game documentation lookup.
Avoid replacing vanilla on_actions files; create mod-prefixed files under common/on_actions/.
Use scripted_effects for repeated OA payloads so scope assumptions are reviewed once.

topics.title: On action general startup and pulse hooks
topics.body: General hooks: on_startup runs on the first day of a new game after country selection, not when loading saves, and has no default country scope; manually scope into countries, states, or other objects before country/state effects. on_daily runs every day for every country separately and is performance-heavy. on_daily_TAG runs daily only for the specified tag if that country exists. on_weekly runs weekly for every country and is useful for AI or maintenance logic. on_weekly_TAG runs weekly for one tag. on_monthly runs monthly for every country and often uses random_events. on_monthly_TAG runs monthly for one tag. Because non-TAG pulses iterate countries, scoping into a fixed tag inside on_daily can repeat the effect once per country on the map.
syntax_blocks: on_startup = { effect = { ENG = { country_event = { id = my_mod.1 days = 365 } } } }
on_daily = { effect = { if = { limit = { has_variable = my_mod_counter } add_to_variable = { my_mod_counter = 1 } } } }
on_daily_SOV = { effect = { if = { limit = { has_war_with = GER } SOV_escalate_the_war_effect = yes } } }
on_weekly = { effect = { if = { limit = { has_intelligence_agency = yes is_ai = yes } update_operation_ai = yes } } }
relationships: on_startup default scope is none; use tag scopes such as ENG = { ... } before country effects.
on_daily/on_weekly/on_monthly default scope is the iterated country.
TAG pulse variants only execute if the country exists.
random_events in pulse hooks evaluates event triggers in the hook scope.
validation: Prefer TAG variants for country-specific mechanics.
Never put broad every_country logic inside on_daily unless the multiplicative cost is intentional.
For GUI counters, prefer daily/weekly variables with narrow flags and cheap checks.
Use 0-weight random_events entries when most pulse checks should do nothing.

topics.title: On action politics, elections, and peace conference hooks
topics.body: Politics hooks: on_stage_coup has ROOT as the country staging the coup and FROM as the target. on_coup_succeeded has ROOT as the country where the coup succeeds and FROM as the coup stager. on_government_change fires when a country changes government and also triggers on_ruling_party_change. on_ruling_party_change fires when ideology changes and exposes old_ideology_token as a temporary token variable. on_new_term_election fires for election events or hold_election. Peace hooks: on_before_peace_conference_start runs after capitulation but before the peace conference and is called for winner/loser pairs. on_peaceconference_started and on_peaceconference_ended use ROOT as winner and FROM as loser; they can also be triggered by white peace or conditional surrender paths.
syntax_blocks: on_stage_coup = { effect = { # ROOT: coup stager; FROM: target country } }
on_coup_succeeded = { effect = { # ROOT: coup country; FROM: coup stager } }
on_before_peace_conference_start = { effect = { # ROOT: winner; FROM: loser } }
relationships: on_government_change and on_ruling_party_change can both fire for the same political transition.
old_ideology_token is useful for generated branching after ideology changes.
Peace conference hooks operate on winner/loser pairs rather than a single global peace context.
validation: Avoid running the same reward in both on_government_change and on_ruling_party_change without a flag gate.
Document whether a peace hook should affect winner, loser, or both.
When using random_events for elections, ensure each event trigger is valid in the country scope.

topics.title: On action diplomacy, war, release, and access hooks
topics.body: War and diplomacy hooks: on_send_volunteers uses ROOT sender and FROM receiver. on_border_war_lost has default scope as the state that lost the scripted border war. on_war_relation_added uses ROOT attacker and FROM defender when two countries become directly at war. on_declare_war uses ROOT declarer and FROM target. on_war has THIS as the country that just entered war from peace. on_peace has THIS as the country that is no longer at war. on_capitulation and on_capitulation_immediate use ROOT capitulated country and FROM winner. on_uncapitulation uses ROOT as the affected country. on_annex uses ROOT winner and FROM annexed country. on_civil_war_end_before_annexation, on_civil_war_end, and on_annex can all participate in civil-war endings, usually with ROOT winner and FROM annexed side. on_puppet uses ROOT puppeted nation and FROM overlord. on_liberate uses ROOT liberated nation and FROM liberator leader. on_release_as_free uses ROOT released free nation and FROM releaser. on_release_as_puppet uses ROOT released puppet and FROM overlord. Access and aid hooks include on_guarantee ROOT guarantor FROM guaranteed, on_military_access ROOT requester FROM acceptor, on_offer_military_access ROOT offerer FROM acceptor, on_call_allies ROOT caller FROM joined ally, on_join_allies ROOT joining country FROM war owner, on_lend_lease ROOT sender FROM acceptor, on_incoming_lend_lease ROOT acceptor FROM requester, on_send_expeditionary_force ROOT sender FROM acceptor, on_return_expeditionary_forces ROOT force owner FROM host, on_request_expeditionary_forces ROOT requester FROM target, on_ask_for_state_control ROOT requester FROM current controller, on_give_state_control ROOT giver FROM receiver, on_peace_proposal ROOT conditional surrender sender FROM receiver, and on_send_attache default sender with FROM receiver.
syntax_blocks: on_war_relation_added = { effect = { # ROOT: attacker; FROM: defender } }
on_declare_war = { effect = { if = { limit = { tag = GER FROM = { tag = SOV } } add_ideas = GER_barbarossa } } }
on_capitulation = { effect = { # ROOT: capitulated country; FROM: winner } }
on_lend_lease = { effect = { # ROOT: sender; FROM: acceptor } }
relationships: on_war_relation_added differs from on_war: relation-added fires for country pairs entering war, while on_war fires when a country moves from peace to war.
Capitulation hooks differ by timing: immediate is at the beginning of capitulation, on_capitulation is mid-process after some cleanup has already occurred.
Release and puppet hooks often require localisation, flags, and cosmetic tag handling.
validation: Do not assume on_war gives the opponent; use on_war_relation_added or on_declare_war when attacker/defender matters.
For civil war endings, guard duplicate effects because annex and civil-war hooks can both fire.
Document sender/receiver direction for every diplomacy hook before generating effects.
Avoid applying country effects in state default scope such as on_border_war_lost without scoping to owner/controller.

topics.title: On action faction, autonomy, and government-in-exile hooks
topics.body: Faction hooks: on_create_faction fires when a country creates a faction, with FROM as the country that joins where supplied. on_faction_formed fires when a faction is formed. on_offer_join_faction has FROM as invited country and THIS/ROOT as faction leader. on_join_faction has FROM as faction leader and THIS/ROOT as joining country. on_assume_faction_leadership has ROOT as new faction leader and FROM as old leader. on_leave_faction has ROOT as country leaving and FROM as faction leader. Autonomy hooks: on_subject_annexed, on_subject_free, and on_subject_autonomy_level_change use ROOT as subject and FROM as overlord or previous overlord. Government-in-exile hooks: on_government_exiled uses ROOT government in exile and FROM host; on_host_changed_from_capitulation uses ROOT government in exile, FROM new host, and FROM.FROM old host; on_exile_government_reinstated uses ROOT returned government and FROM previous host.
syntax_blocks: on_offer_join_faction = { effect = { # ROOT/THIS: faction leader; FROM: invited country } }
on_join_faction = { effect = { # ROOT/THIS: joining country; FROM: faction leader } }
on_subject_autonomy_level_change = { effect = { # ROOT: subject; FROM: overlord } }
on_host_changed_from_capitulation = { effect = { # ROOT: exile government; FROM: new host; FROM.FROM: old host } }
relationships: Faction offer and join hooks reverse the meaning of ROOT/FROM compared with each other.
Subject hooks are useful for autonomy systems but should not assume peace-conference context.
Exile hooks are three-party when host changes from capitulation.
validation: State ROOT/FROM direction in comments for faction hooks because offer/join naming is easy to misread.
Gate subject autonomy effects by autonomy state if only a specific level matters.
Use FROM.FROM only in hooks documented to provide it, such as host change from capitulation.

topics.title: On action state-control and wargoal hooks
topics.body: State hook: on_state_control_changed fires when a state's controller changes; ROOT is new controller, FROM is old controller, and FROM.FROM is the state. Wargoal hooks: on_generate_wargoal uses ROOT as wargoal owner and FROM as target. on_justifying_wargoal_pulse checks daily while a wargoal is being justified; FROM is target nation. on_wargoal_expire uses FROM as wargoal owner in the documented pattern. These hooks are common sources of scope errors because state effects must be put inside FROM.FROM or another state scope.
syntax_blocks: on_state_control_changed = { effect = { if = { limit = { FROM.FROM = { state = 123 } } FROM.FROM = { set_resistance = 60 } } } }
on_generate_wargoal = { effect = { # ROOT: wargoal owner; FROM: target } }
relationships: State-control hooks are a safe place to rename states, set resistance, or trigger country effects after checking state ID.
Wargoal pulse hooks can be expensive because they are checked frequently.
FROM.FROM in on_state_control_changed is a state scope, not a country.
validation: Never run state-only effects directly in ROOT for on_state_control_changed.
Check state ID or owner/controller before applying broad effects.
For justification pulses, use event triggers and flags to prevent repeated event spam.
Document whether FROM is country target or wargoal owner for each wargoal hook.

topics.title: On action unit leader, military, and ace hooks
topics.body: Unit leader hooks: on_unit_leader_created, on_army_leader_daily, on_army_leader_won_combat, on_army_leader_lost_combat, on_unit_leader_level_up, and on_army_leader_promoted use ROOT as unit leader and FROM as owner country. on_unit_leader_promote_from_ranks_veteran and on_unit_leader_promote_from_ranks_green use ROOT as unit leader, FROM as unit, and OWNER as owner country. Military hooks: on_nuke_drop uses ROOT launching country and FROM nuked state. on_pride_of_the_fleet_sunk uses ROOT as country that lost the pride ship and FROM as killer country. on_naval_invasion and on_paradrop have THIS/default as invaded state, ROOT as invading country, and FROM as source state; on_units_paradropped_in_state uses ROOT as destination state and FROM as dropping country. on_add_history uses ROOT as unit. Ace hooks: on_ace_promoted has FROM as ace. on_ace_killed, on_ace_killed_on_accident, and on_non_ace_killed_other_ace use FROM for the relevant ace. on_ace_killed_by_ace and on_ace_killed_other_ace use FROM and PREV to represent the two aces depending on survivor/death side. on_aces_killed_each_other uses FROM ace and PREV enemy ace.
syntax_blocks: on_army_leader_daily = { effect = { # ROOT: unit leader; FROM: owner country } }
on_unit_leader_promote_from_ranks_veteran = { effect = { # ROOT: unit leader; FROM: unit; OWNER: owner country } }
relationships: Unit leader hooks are character/unit-leader scoped, not country scoped.
Naval invasion and paradrop explicitly separate THIS/default state from ROOT invading country.
Ace duel hooks use PREV for the second ace; generated events should preserve that chain.
validation: Scope into FROM before owner-country effects in unit leader hooks.
Do not use country effects directly in on_naval_invasion or on_paradrop default state scope.
For ace events, document which ace is FROM and which is PREV before writing localisation or effects.
Use timed traits carefully in leader promotion hooks to avoid stacking.

topics.title: On action La Resistance intelligence and MIO hooks
topics.body: La Resistance hooks: on_operation_completed uses THIS as operation, ROOT as initiating country, and FROM as target country. on_operative_detected_during_operation uses THIS operative, ROOT killer country when present, FROM operating country, and FROM.FROM operation state when a specific selection target exists. on_operative_on_mission_spotted uses THIS operative, FROM mission country, and ROOT operating country. on_operative_captured uses THIS operative, ROOT country where captured, and FROM operating country. on_operative_created and on_operative_recruited use THIS operative and FROM creator country. on_operative_death uses THIS operative, optional ROOT killer country, and FROM operating country. on_fully_decrypted_cipher uses THIS target country and FROM decrypter country. on_activated_active_decryption_bonuses uses THIS target country and FROM activating country. MIO hooks: on_mio_size_increased, on_mio_design_team_assigned_to_tech, on_mio_design_team_assigned_to_variant, on_mio_industrial_manufacturer_assigned, and on_mio_industrial_manufacturer_unassigned use ROOT as Military Industrial Organization and FROM as owner country.
syntax_blocks: on_operation_completed = { effect = { # THIS: operation; ROOT: initiating country; FROM: target country } }
on_operative_captured = { effect = { # THIS: operative; ROOT: country where captured; FROM: operating country } }
on_fully_decrypted_cipher = { effect = { # THIS: target country; FROM: decrypter country } }
on_mio_size_increased = { effect = { # ROOT: MIO; FROM: owner country } }
relationships: Operation and operative hooks often use THIS for non-country objects, so country effects must scope to ROOT or FROM.
FROM.FROM in operative detection is only present when the operation has a specific selection target state.
MIO hooks are object scoped and must scope through FROM for owner-country effects.
validation: Check DLC assumptions before generating La Resistance or MIO hook content.
Do not assume ROOT is always available in operative death; killer country can be optional.
When FROM.FROM may be absent, guard state logic with limits or avoid mandatory state effects.
For MIO hooks, document whether the effect mutates the organization object or its owner country.

topics.title: Decision categories and decisions
topics.body: Decision categories group decisions; individual decisions control visibility, availability, cost, timers, removal, effects, and AI use. Generated decisions must distinguish visible from available so unavailable choices can still be intentionally shown.
syntax_blocks: my_category = { icon = generic_decisions my_decision = { icon = generic_decision cost = 25 available = { always = yes } complete_effect = { add_political_power = -25 } } }
relationships: Decisions consume triggers and effects.
Localisation keys usually match category and decision IDs.
validation: Use visible for display gates and available for click gates.
Set fire_only_once or days_remove where relevant.
Check AI weights.
Hide helper-only triggers, effects, or calculation modifiers from player-facing UI whenever the file type supports a hidden form.

topics.title: Missions, timers, and recurring decisions
topics.body: Timed decisions and missions run after a duration or disappear after conditions change. They need clear timeout, cancellation, and repeat semantics so generated content does not loop or disappear unexpectedly.
relationships: Timed decisions often drive event chains.
AI behavior controls repeated use.
validation: Define timeout behavior.
Avoid repeatable cost exploits.
Use flags or variables for chain state.

topics.title: Decision, mission, targeted decision, and state target block differences
topics.body: 普通决议 is selected by the player or AI and usually gates use with visible/available/allowed before running complete_effect. 任务 uses the same decision file family but becomes a timed mission when days_mission_timeout is present: activation starts the timer, available describes the success condition, complete_effect runs on success, and timeout_effect runs on failure. targeted_decision adds a country target; in target filters and effects, ROOT is the owner country and FROM is the target country. state_target or target_state style blocks add a state target; when a secondary state scope exists, generated code must document whether FROM.FROM or a named event target is the state. Do not reuse ordinary decision semantics blindly inside missions.
syntax_blocks: my_category = { my_decision = { visible = { has_country_flag = my_mod_unlocked } available = { has_political_power > 25 } cost = 25 complete_effect = { add_political_power = -25 } } }

my_category = { my_mission = { activation = { has_war = yes } available = { controls_state = 64 } days_mission_timeout = 100 is_good = yes complete_effect = { add_political_power = 50 } timeout_effect = { add_political_power = -50 } } }
targeted_decision = { target_root_trigger = { has_war = yes } target_trigger = { is_neighbor_of = ROOT } complete_effect = { create_wargoal = { type = annex_everything target = FROM } } }
state_target = { target_root_trigger = { always = yes } target_trigger = { is_owned_by = ROOT } complete_effect = { FROM.FROM = { add_core_of = ROOT } } }
relationships: Decision categories can have their own allowed/visible/priority logic before individual decisions are considered.
Missions and targeted missions are defined by days_mission_timeout and need timeout/cancel semantics.
Targeted decisions use FROM for the selected country target, which can also be referenced in localisation as [From.GetNameDef].
State-targeted logic is more performance-sensitive because target_trigger may be evaluated across many states.
validation: For ordinary decisions, distinguish visible as display gating and available as click gating.
For missions, treat activation as start condition and available as objective/success condition.
Use cancel_trigger/cancel_effect only when cancellation should differ from timeout or success.
Document ROOT/FROM/FROM.FROM in every targeted decision or mission plan.
Use fixed_random_seed = no when a repeatable decision intentionally needs fresh random_list results.
Avoid expensive target_trigger scans; prefer activate_targeted_decision when the target is already known.

topics.title: Ideas, advisors, and national spirits
topics.body: Idea files define national spirits, advisors, manufacturers, theorists, and other idea types. The key content is picture, allowed/visible/available logic, cost, traits, and modifier blocks.
syntax_blocks: ideas = { country = { my_idea = { picture = my_idea modifier = { stability_factor = 0.05 } } } }
relationships: Ideas wrap modifiers.
Characters can own advisor roles.
Localisation keys match idea IDs.
validation: Use valid idea category.
Keep picture key available.
Do not mix incompatible advisor fields.
Hide helper-only triggers, effects, or calculation modifiers from player-facing UI whenever the file type supports a hidden form.

topics.title: Dynamic modifiers and scripted value-driven buffs
topics.body: Dynamic modifiers let script attach a reusable modifier block to a country or state. They are useful for systems where effects add, remove, or update persistent buffs without creating many ideas.
relationships: Effects attach dynamic modifiers.
Variables can drive scripted values related to modifiers.
validation: Remove old dynamic modifiers before reapplying when necessary.
Keep modifier scope compatible.
Localise displayed modifiers if visible.

topics.title: Characters, leaders, advisors, and roles
topics.body: Characters define political leaders, advisors, field marshals, corps commanders, navy leaders, and other roles. Generated characters need portraits, traits, ideology, country ownership, and recruitment or history integration.
syntax_blocks: characters = { my_character = { name = my_character_name portraits = { civilian = { large = GFX_portrait_my_character } } country_leader = { ideology = neutrality traits = { } } } }
relationships: Ideas can reference character advisors.
History files recruit or set leaders.
Traits provide modifiers.
validation: Ensure portrait GFX exists.
Use valid role block.
Localise character names.

topics.title: Leader and advisor traits
topics.body: Traits define modifiers and constraints for leaders, generals, admirals, and advisors. Generated traits should be tied to the correct character role and should not reuse vanilla IDs unless intentionally modifying them.
relationships: Traits are assigned inside character role blocks.
Trait modifiers share modifier scope rules.
validation: Use role-compatible trait files.
Localise trait name and description.
Check modifier keys.

topics.title: Country tags and country creation
topics.body: Country creation involves tag registration, country history, color, graphical culture, flags, localisation, and optionally cosmetic tags. Generated content should treat the three-letter tag as a namespace root.
relationships: History, focus trees, ideas, and localisation all reference tags.
Cosmetic tags alter display identity.
validation: Use unused tags.
Add localisation for country and adjective.
Ensure flag assets exist.

topics.title: Cosmetic tags and dynamic country names
topics.body: Cosmetic tags change a country's visible name, flag, and identity without changing its underlying tag. They are useful for government changes, civil war outcomes, and scripted narrative states.
relationships: Cosmetic tags require localisation and flags.
Events and focuses commonly set cosmetic tags.
validation: Provide fallback country localisation.
Do not treat cosmetic tag as a real country tag in history files.
Check flag naming.

topics.title: Country history setup
topics.body: Country history files set starting politics, capital, technology, OOB references, characters, ideas, and starting effects. Generated setup must be deterministic and compatible with the selected start date.
relationships: Country history references states, OOB files, characters, ideas, and technologies.
validation: Use existing state IDs.
Check recruited character IDs.
Keep start-date blocks explicit.

topics.title: State history, ownership, cores, and buildings
topics.body: State history defines owner, controller, cores, claims, buildings, victory points, resources, and province-level history. Generated state edits are conflict-prone and should be scoped to explicit state IDs.
syntax_blocks: history = { owner = TAG add_core_of = TAG buildings = { infrastructure = 3 arms_factory = 2 } victory_points = { 1234 10 } }
relationships: Country history needs valid capital state.
Map and strategic regions contextualize state IDs.
validation: Avoid replacing whole state files unless intended.
Check owner/controller consistency.
Keep victory point province IDs valid.

topics.title: OOB files and starting units
topics.body: Order of battle files define starting divisions, ships, air wings, and templates. Generated OOB must reference valid province IDs, equipment, division templates, and technology availability.
syntax_blocks: division = { name = "1st Division" location = 1234 division_template = "Infantry Division" start_experience_factor = 0.1 }
relationships: Country history loads OOB.
Division templates and equipment archetypes must exist.
validation: Check province IDs.
Keep template names exact.
Avoid giving equipment that does not exist.

topics.title: Map provinces, terrain, and continent data
topics.body: Map modding covers province definitions, terrain, continents, heightmaps, rivers, and related files. It is a high-risk domain because visual files and CSV data must agree exactly.
relationships: States reference provinces.
Strategic regions and supply systems rely on map data.
validation: Do not generate map edits without explicit user confirmation.
Keep province IDs unique.
Validate CSV and bitmap consistency externally.

topics.title: Adjacencies, strategic regions, and supply map
topics.body: Adjacencies define special map connections; strategic regions group provinces for air/naval logic; supply nodes and railways define logistics. Generated content must cross-check province IDs and region membership.
relationships: Air regions and naval regions affect AI and combat.
State edits interact with supply and strategic regions.
validation: Check province IDs exist.
Avoid duplicate region membership.
Do not infer supply topology from state ownership alone.

topics.title: Technology trees and research
topics.body: Technology files define researchable items, folders, paths, prerequisites, bonuses, and equipment unlocks. Generated techs must integrate with folders, icons, localisation, and equipment or unit systems.
syntax_blocks: technologies = { my_tech = { area = industry start_year = 1936 cost = 1.0 } }
relationships: Technologies unlock equipment and modify units.
Focuses and decisions can grant research bonuses.
validation: Check folder and area.
Use valid start_year and cost.
Localise tech name and description.

topics.title: Equipment archetypes and variants
topics.body: Equipment files define archetypes, concrete equipment, stats, build cost, year, parent relationships, and interface category. Generated equipment must be unlocked by tech or history and referenced by units or OOB.
syntax_blocks: equipments = { my_equipment = { year = 1936 archetype = infantry_equipment build_cost_ic = 0.5 } }
relationships: Technologies unlock equipment.
OOB and units consume equipment.
GFX and localisation expose equipment to UI.
validation: Check archetype exists.
Use plausible year.
Add localisation and icons.

topics.title: Division templates and land units
topics.body: Division templates define regiment layout, support companies, priority, and displayed names. Generated templates must match available subunits and equipment, and OOB divisions must reference exact template names.
syntax_blocks: division_template = { name = "Infantry Division" regiments = { infantry = { x = 0 y = 0 } } support = { engineer = { x = 0 y = 0 } } }
relationships: OOB divisions use templates.
Subunits define equipment and stats.
Technologies unlock battalions.
validation: Check battalion names.
Keep template names consistent.
Avoid impossible equipment requirements.

topics.title: Sub-units, battalions, and support companies
topics.body: Sub-units define battalion and support company stats, categories, equipment needs, map icons, and combat behavior. Generated subunits are gameplay-balance sensitive and require technology and equipment integration.
syntax_blocks: sub_units = { my_battalion = { sprite = infantry type = infantry group = infantry combat_width = 2 } }
relationships: Division templates reference subunits.
Equipment and technology gate availability.
validation: Check equipment needs.
Avoid duplicate category errors.
Test combat width and AI usage.

topics.title: Buildings, resources, and state modifiers
topics.body: Buildings and resources are commonly set in state history or modified by effects. Generated economy content should distinguish permanent state setup from scripted construction and temporary modifiers.
relationships: State history sets initial values.
Effects can add construction or slots.
Modifiers alter output factors.
validation: Use valid building keys.
Keep resource values numeric.
Avoid state file replacement unless intended.

topics.title: GUI layout and GFX sprite registration
topics.body: GUI layout files define windows and controls; GFX files register sprites and images. Generated GUI must keep element names, scripted_gui references, GFX names, and asset paths aligned.
relationships: Scripted GUI triggers/effects/properties reference GUI element names.
Localisation provides text for textboxes.
validation: Check GFX_ names match.
Check asset path exists.
Avoid overlapping UI positions.
Generated comments should be in clear English and explain only non-obvious logic.

topics.title: Scripted GUI dynamic lists and properties
topics.body: Scripted GUI binds GUI windows to script-driven visibility, click effects, dynamic properties, and dynamic lists. It is the core bridge for custom HOI4 interfaces and must be generated with exact element names.
syntax_blocks: scripted_gui = { my_gui = { context_type = player_context window_name = "my_window" visible = { always = yes } dynamic_lists = { my_grid = { array = ROOT.my_array entry_container = my_entry change_scope = no value = my_value index = my_index } } } }
relationships: GUI element names must match triggers, effects, and properties.
Arrays and temp variables drive dynamic lists and positions.
GFX registers images used by properties.
validation: Check window_name exists in .gui.
Use cheap visible triggers.
Clear and rebuild temp arrays before dynamic list calculations.
Generated comments should be in clear English and explain only non-obvious logic.

topics.title: Interface assets, fonts, and sprite sheets
topics.body: Interface assets include textures, fonts, sprite sheets, and definitions for GUI controls. Generated asset references should be stable and mod-local to avoid collisions with vanilla interface files.
relationships: GUI controls consume GFX sprites.
Localisation text must fit textboxes.
Asset dimensions influence layout.
validation: Avoid referencing missing textures.
Check text width and maxWidth.
Use mod-specific asset directories.

topics.title: AI strategies and scripted AI behavior
topics.body: AI strategy files and ai_will_do blocks steer how AI countries pick focuses, decisions, diplomacy, production, and plans. Generated AI logic should be conservative and debuggable.
syntax_blocks: ai_will_do = { factor = 1 modifier = { factor = 0 has_war = yes } }
ai_strategy = { type = conquer id = TAG value = 50 }
relationships: Focuses and decisions use ai_will_do.
Country AI strategies interact with diplomacy and war goals.
validation: Avoid unconditional extreme factors.
Gate AI behavior by tag or situation.
Test AI-only runs.

topics.title: Diplomacy, opinion, factions, and autonomy
topics.body: Diplomacy scripting covers opinions, factions, guarantees, war goals, autonomy state, subjects, and scripted diplomatic effects. Generated content must distinguish target and actor scopes.
relationships: Opinion modifiers need definitions and localisation.
War goals and factions often come from events or focuses.
validation: Check target scopes.
Localise opinion modifiers and faction names.
Avoid invalid autonomy state IDs.

topics.title: Game rules and startup configuration
topics.body: Game rules expose startup choices that can alter country setup, AI behavior, or mod systems. Generated game rules need clear localisation, default choices, and script hooks that read the selected option.
syntax_blocks: game_rule = { name = my_rule group = my_group option = { name = default } }
relationships: Startup events and on_actions read game rules.
Localisation names rule groups and options.
validation: Provide a default option.
Do not require DLC-specific behavior unless documented.
Use rule checks in generated triggers.

topics.title: Defines and game constants
topics.body: Defines override game constants and affect broad systems. They are powerful but risky for compatibility. Generated mods should avoid defines unless the user explicitly requests balance or engine-level tuning.
relationships: Defines can affect every generated content system.
Latest patch snapshots matter for define compatibility.
validation: Do not generate defines by default.
Keep overrides minimal.
Verify define names against current game version.

topics.title: Bookmarks and start dates
topics.body: Bookmarks define scenario selection, highlighted countries, start dates, and visible descriptions. Generated bookmarks must match history setup and localisation.
syntax_blocks: bookmarks = { bookmark = { date = 1936.1.1 default = yes selectable = { TAG } } }
relationships: Country and state history may contain date-specific blocks.
Bookmarks depend on country tags and localisation.
validation: Ensure start date has matching history.
Localise bookmark and country descriptions.
Check selectable tags exist.

topics.title: Sound, music, and asset registration
topics.body: Audio modding registers music, sound effects, and asset files. Generated references should keep file paths relative, include required metadata, and avoid replacing vanilla audio unintentionally.
relationships: Events and GUI can trigger sounds.
Music stations need localisation and asset files.
validation: Check audio files exist.
Use supported formats.
Avoid duplicate sound names.

topics.title: Common generation and loading errors
topics.body: Common generated-file failures include missing braces, extra equals signs, wrong folders, missing localisation BOM, mismatched GFX names, invalid scope assumptions, duplicate IDs, missing assets, and hidden vanilla content caused by replace_path. RHoiScribe can classify error.log into subsystems and connect messages to changed paths. For crash or load-failure debugging, summarize the error categories first, then inspect only likely changed files instead of blindly rewriting the mod.
syntax_blocks: validate_hoi4_debug_run { game_path = "<GAME_PATH>" document_path = "<HOI4_DOCUMENT_PATH>" workspace_mod_path = "<MOD_ROOT>" launch = false }
relationships: Every generator should surface path, ID, encoding, and scope assumptions.
Error logs should guide targeted fixes instead of blind regeneration.
Debug launch checks should run before starting hoi4.exe with -gdpr-compliant -debug_mode.
validation: Run game with debug logs for release-critical content.
Search error.log by generated prefix.
Check localisation and GFX names when UI text or sprites are missing.
Classify error.log before proposing fixes after a crash.
Use git diff or status only to analyze which generated files changed unless the user permits modifications.
Before debug launch, confirm the document map, localisation, and history folders are empty so nudge output or stale files do not contaminate the run.

topics.title: Agent delivery, language, and workspace safety rules
topics.body: Agents should keep the conversation in the user initial language, while new code comments should be concise English comments. Generated deliverables should be complete, game-readable content rather than skeletons, placeholders, TODO blocks, or follow-up stubs. Workspace file names, folder names, token-like script IDs, OOB division names, variables, flags, idea IDs, focus IDs, event IDs, and similar machine-readable fields should be ASCII-only; localisation prose and visible player text may use the target language. Agents must not touch unrelated workspace content, leave temporary ps1 scripts or other helper files behind, reset git state, or commit without user permission.
syntax_blocks: # Calculate dynamic row offset for each visible entry.
git commit -m "feat: add automated release binaries"
relationships: Unique identifier scanning enforces creation-time safety.
Localisation topics define the player-facing language exception.
Debug topics explain when git analysis is appropriate.
validation: Check generated paths and token fields for ASCII before writing.
Remove temporary helper files after use.
When committing is allowed, use Conventional Commits and include only files related to the current step.
Do not claim completion until generated content is usable without a later fill-in pass.

topics.title: Game path discovery and debug launch preflight
topics.body: When local game context is needed, discover the HOI4 install through Steam first: read SteamPath or InstallPath, read steamapps/libraryfolders.vdf, find steamapps/appmanifest_394360.acf, parse installdir, then validate steamapps/common/<installdir>. If Steam metadata fails, scan candidate roots for a folder named Hearts of Iron IV and validate it by requiring hoi4.exe plus common, history, events, localisation, and map. launcher-settings.json in the game directory can provide gameDataPath for the HOI4 document folder and version for patch-aware decisions. Before launching hoi4.exe with -gdpr-compliant -debug_mode, validate that the document map, localisation, and history folders are empty; the document mod/*.mod descriptors exist; the workspace mod descriptor path points to the current workspace with / slashes; and dlc_load.json enables only the workspace mod plus dependencies.
syntax_blocks: validate_hoi4_debug_run { game_path = "<GAME_PATH>" document_path = "<HOI4_DOCUMENT_PATH>" workspace_mod_path = "<MOD_ROOT>" launch = false }
relationships: Debug runs should be followed by classify_error_log when the game reports errors or crashes.
Dependencies in descriptor.mod determine which extra .mod descriptors may be enabled.
launcher-settings.json version can guide bundled knowledge and compatibility checks.
validation: Steam discovery must validate the final game path before returning it.
Document path checks target the HOI4 user data folder, not the game install folder.
Do not launch when any preflight check is red unless the user explicitly accepts the risk.
Use / slashes in launcher .mod path fields pointing to the workspace mod.
